coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of efficient alginate composite beads embedded with N-doped carbon dots and their application for enhanced rare earth elements adsorption from aqueous solutions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zhiwei
Li, Quan
Li, Zhiyue
Liu, Cong
Liu, Xuerui
Liu, Yuanyuan
Dong, Genlai
Lan, Tao
Wei, Yun
coredata.dc:description: The increasing demand of rare earth elements in agriculture and industry raises the potential environmental pollution with their inevitable toxicity and biological accumulation. It is necessary to develop an efficient and low-cost technique to effectively remove rare earth elements. N-doped carbon dots (CDs) were green-synthesized using one-pot domestic microwave oven and novel alginate composite (ALG@CDs) beads were fabricated by embedding the N-doped CDs into pure alginate hydrogel beads. The synthetic materials were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, high resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis, and zeta potential measurement. The ALG@CDs beads could effectively adsorb rare earth elements from aqueous solutions. Taking the adsorption of rare earth element Gd (III) as an example, initial concentration, adsorbent dosage, pH, temperature and contact time were optimized. The adsorption isotherms and kinetics fitted to Langmuir isotherm model (R2 > 0.9991) and pseudo-second-order kinetic model (R2 = 0.9918), respectively. The adsorption capacity of ALG@CDs-3 (201.21 mg/g) for Gd (III) was much higher than that of bare ALG beads (124.38 mg/g) according to the maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of Langmuir model. Competitive adsorption results indicated that ALG@CDs beads had stronger affinity to Gd (III) than to Al (III), Co (II), Ni (II), Zn (II), and Mg (II). The adsorbent could be easily collected and reused for five adsorption-regeneration cycles. The results indicated that the fabricated ALG@CDs beads are the promising materials for adsorption or enrichment of rare earth elements from waste water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Alginate
Rare earth elements
Adsorption

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of calcium alginate beads (ALG beads)
Fabrication of carbon dots embedded in calcium alginate beads (ALG@CDs beads)
Effect of the ALG@CDs-3 bead dosage
Effect of the initial pH on adsorption efficiency of Gd (III)
Effect of doping amounts of CDs and initial concentration

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China
Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of ALG beads (a); ALG@CDs-3 beads (b); Gd (III)-loaded ALG@CDs-3 beads (c); CDs (d).
SEM images of ALG beads (A); ALG@CDs-3 beads (B) and HRTEM of carbon dots (C, D).
The XPS survey spectra (A); Gd 4d spectra (B); N 1s spectra (C); Ca 2p spectra (D); O 1s spectra (E); C 1s spectra (F) of ALG@CDs-3 beads (a), Gd (III)-loaded ALG@CDs-3 beads (b), ALG beads (c).
TGA analysis curve of ALG beads (a); ALG@CDs-3 beads (b); carbon dots (c).
Effect of doses (A) and pH (B) for Gd (III) adsorption on ALG@CDs-3 beads.

Effects of initial concentration for Gd (III) adsorption on ALG@CDs beads (A); Dy (III), Sm (III), Pr (III) on ALG@CDs-3 beads (D) and adsorption isotherms of Langmuir (B, E), Freundlich models (C, F).
Effects of contact time for Gd (III) adsorption on ALG@CDs-3 beads (A) and adsorption kinetics of PFO (B), PSO (C) models.
Effects of temperature for Gd (III) adsorption on ALG@CDs-3 beads (A) and Van’t Hoff plots (B).
Reuse of ALG@CDs-3 beads in removal of Gd (III) from aqueous solution.
Competitive adsorption of metal ions with Gd (III) binary mixtures on ALG@CDs-3 beads.
Isotherm Parameters for Gd (III) adsorption on ALG@CDs beads.
Isotherm Parameters for Dy (III) Sm (III) Pr (III) adsorption on ALG@CDs-3 beads.
Kinetic Parameters for Gd (III) adsorption on ALG@CDs-3 beads.
Thermodynamic parameters of Gd (III) adsorption on ALG@CDs-3 beads.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of efficient alginate composite beads embedded with N-doped carbon dots and their application for enhanced rare earth elements adsorption from aqueous solutions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, 15 3rd North East Road, Chaoyang District, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: tate Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, 15 3rd North East Road, Chaoyang District, Beijing 100029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China National Institute of Standardization, Zhong guancun South Avenue, Haidian District, Beijing 100081, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China National Institute of Standardization, Zhong guancun South Avenue, Haidian District, Beijing 100081, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasing demand of rare earth elements in agriculture and industry raises the potential environmental pollution with their inevitable toxicity and biological accumulation. It is necessary to develop an efficient and low-cost technique to effectively remove rare earth elements. N-doped carbon dots (CDs) were green-synthesized using one-pot domestic microwave oven and novel alginate composite (ALG@CDs) beads were fabricated by embedding the N-doped CDs into pure alginate hydrogel beads. The synthetic materials were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, high resolution transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis, and zeta potential measurement. The ALG@CDs beads could effectively adsorb rare earth elements from aqueous solutions. Taking the adsorption of rare earth element Gd (III) as an example, initial concentration, adsorbent dosage, pH, temperature and contact time were optimized. The adsorption isotherms and kinetics fitted to Langmuir isotherm model (R2 > 0.9991) and pseudo-second-order kinetic model (R2 = 0.9918), respectively. The adsorption capacity of ALG@CDs-3 (201.21 mg/g) for Gd (III) was much higher than that of bare ALG beads (124.38 mg/g) according to the maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of Langmuir model. Competitive adsorption results indicated that ALG@CDs beads had stronger affinity to Gd (III) than to Al (III), Co (II), Ni (II), Zn (II), and Mg (II). The adsorbent could be easily collected and reused for five adsorption-regeneration cycles. The results indicated that the fabricated ALG@CDs beads are the promising materials for adsorption or enrichment of rare earth elements from waste water.

ce:section.ce:para: Rare earth elements (REEs) are promising components in modern industries, such as batteries [1], catalyst [2], photology [3], and electronic [4] industries, owing to their distinctive catalytic, optical, and magnetic properties. With the progress of the science and technology, the demand for REEs is increasing sharply. REE minerals are non-renewable resources and distributed unevenly in the world [5]. However, waste water with REE ions is a potential problem for environment and human health because of their toxicity [6]. Long-term exposure or ingestion of REEs may have adverse effect on health and metabolism due to accumulating in the corporal organs [7]. There are some techniques, including ion exchange [8], chemical precipitation [9], electrolysis [10], and adsorption [11], for separating and enriching the REEs from industrial or urban effluents. Bioadsorption is generally considered as an environment friendly, effectiveness, and low cost technology for the adsorption of the REE ions, compared with the solvent extraction that requires a large amount of toxic organic solvent [12].

Calcium alginate gel beads, nature biosorbent, cross-linked by sodium alginate (SA) and calcium ion, are consistent with 'egg-box model' [13]. SA could be extracted from brown seaweed as a natural and renewable polysaccharide polymer material, possessing bountiful carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups [14]. Furthermore, SA aqueous solution has the capability of chelating with divalent or multivalent metal ions by –OH or –COOH in the chain, forming an insoluble SA hydrogel [13]. Owing to its nontoxicity, biocompatibility, easy separation, strong affinity, and hydrophilic properties [15–17], SA has a wide range of applications in drug slow-release [18], effluent treatment [19], tissue repair [20], and adsorption [21]. In order to improve adsorption capacity for metal ions, many researchers have made great efforts to create diverse functional groups by the manner of grafting or blending. Yan et al. prepared the alginate@polyethylenimine sorbent by grafting alginate with polyethylenimine using glutaraldehyde as crosslink agent for Cr (VI) adsorption from aqueous solutions [22]. Song et al. prepared a novel adsorbent by employing ZIF-8/calcium alginate microparticles, which afforded a maximum Pb (II) adsorption capacity as high as 1331.21 mg/g [23].

In the past decades, carbon dots (CDs) emerged as a novel zero-dimensional nanostructure carbon material with remarkable properties, such as strong chemical stability, weak toxicity, excellent mechanical strength, superior photoluminescence, and solubility [24–26]. Due to these attractive characters, CDs have excellent performance in various chemical or biology fields such as biosensors, biological imaging in vivo [27], drug release [28], photochemical catalysis [29], and optical detection [30]. Furthermore, multiple functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH2) make CDs have an extraordinary selective detection and adsorption for metal ions. However, CDs are hard to be collected due to their dimension less than 10 nm, restricts their application in the field of adsorption. To overcome this shortcoming, loading or wrapping CDs on a supporter has been adopted for improving their adsorption performance. Wang et al. reported that the CDs/amino-functionalized ordered mesoporous silica (SBA-NH2) nanocomposite had a higher adsorption to uranium compared with the bare SBA-NH2 [31]. Zhang et al. reported that embedding CDs on the surface of layered double hydroxide could lead to an obvious enhancement in adsorption of methyl blue anionic dye [32]. Up to now, there were no reports on CDs-based alginate materials for REEs adsorption. We supposed that a small amount of CDs doped into SA beads could improve the adsorption of REEs.

The purposes of this work were to synthesize N-doped CDs using a green one-pot domestic microwave oven and novel alginate composite (ALG@CDs) beads by embedding the N-doped CDs into bare alginate hydrogel beads without crosslink agent. The adsorption conditions of ALG@CDs for REE Gd (III) were investigated by regulating the doping amount of CDs, initial pH, temperature, Gd (III) concentration, adsorbent dosage, and contact time. The adsorption performance of ALG@CDs beads in Gd (III) solution mixed with competing metal ions and the cyclic utilization were also evaluated. Finally, the adsorption isotherm and kinetic models and thermodynamic property were explored.

SA, carboxymethyl chitosan (Mw = 220) were supplied from Alfa-Aesar and Macklin Chemical Reagent Company respectively. Calcium chloride anhydrous (96%) and ethylenediamine (99%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Company. HCl and NaOH were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company. All stock solutions were prepared by dissolving the corresponding metal salt in the ultrapure water: Gd(NO3)3·6H2O (99.99%), Sm(NO3)3·6H2O (99.99%), Co(NO3)2·6H2O (99%), Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (99%), Mg(NO3)2·6H2O (99%) were all purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Company. Dy(NO3)3·6H2O (99.99%), Pr(NO3)3·6H2O (99.99%) were acquired from Energy Chemical Company and NiCl2·6H2O (99%), Al(NO3)3·9H2O (99%) were obtained from Macklin Chemical Reagent Company. All the above reagents were used without further purification. The water has a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm−1 (ELGA Centra R200, ELGA Labwater, UK) used as dilution solvent.

N-doped CDs were prepared by microwave method according to the previous report [33]. Typically, 9 mg of carboxymethyl chitosan was fully dissolved in 10 mL of water to form a homogeneous solution, and then 1 mL of 1.0 mol/L HCl was added to chitosan aqueous solution. Subsequently, 700 μL of ethylenediamine was dropped under ultrasonication. The mixed solution was heated for 2 min with a household microwave oven (700 W). The obtained product, which was a red-brown solid, was diluted with 2 mL of water and filtered through a 0.45-μm filtering membrane with a syringe to remove the unreacted substance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of calcium alginate beads (ALG beads)
ce:section.ce:para: To generate a homogeneous viscous solution, SA solution (2%, w/v) was made by dissolving 2 g alginate powder into 100 mL water under magnetic stirring violently for 3 h. ALG beads could be simply generated in a manner that dropped sodium alginate solution into 300 mL 0.1 mol/L CaCl2 solution with the help of magnetic stirring. Keep ALG beads immerse in CaCl2 solution for extra 16 h to confirm the solidification completely. The beads were washed 3 times with abundant water to remove the extra ions and kept in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of carbon dots embedded in calcium alginate beads (ALG@CDs beads)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis procedure of ALG@CDs was according to previous literature with a slightly modification [34] and exhibited in Fig. S1. The prepared ALG beads were dried with filter paper. 10 g of the same size beads were put into 50 mL diluent CDs solution, which was prepared by adding 1 mL, 3 mL, and 10 mL CDs into 49 mL, 47 mL, 40 mL water, respectively, for 6 h. After rinsing properly with abundant water to remove unbound CDs, the ALG@CDs-1, ALG@CDs-3, ALG@CDs-10 beads were produced respectively and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h and further used for REEs adsorption.

Functional groups of the synthetic materials were detected by using Fourier transform infrared spectra Nicolet 8700 (FT-IR, Thermal Fisher, USA) with KBr pellet method. To observe the surface morphology, scanning electron microscopy S-4800 (SEM, Hitachi, Japan) was used. A high resolution transmission electron microscopy JEM-3010 (HRTEM, Hitachi, Japan) and Zetasizer Nano-ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, UK) were applied to characterize the CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy measurements were realized by EscaLab 250Xi (XPS, Thermal Fisher, USA). A 700 W household microwave (M1-L213B, Midea, China) was used to prepare CDs. The concentration of all metal ion solutions was determined by a Perkin Elmer-8000 (ICP-OES, PerkinElmer, USA).

Adsorption performance of ALG@CDs beads was measured as following conditions except for an additional illustration. Afterwards, 20 mg of dry adsorbents were added into 20 mL sample bottle with 10 mL varied initial concentration of REEs solution under constantly shaking at 180 rpm and 298.2 K for a period of 18 h at pH 5.0. The pH of initial concentration was adjusted by 1 mol/L HCl or NaOH solution. Remaining concentration of REEs solution was analyzed by inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES). All experiments have been carried twice in same conditions.

Adsorption capacity
Q t
(mg/g) or
Q e
(mg/g) was computed when adsorption reached at the certain and equilibrium time respectively as following Eqs. (1) and (2):
(1)
Q t
=
C 0
-
C t
V
m
(2)
Q e
=
C 0
-
C e
V
m
where
C 0
,
C t
and
C e
(mg/L) are REEs concentrations at initial, certain, and equilibrium time, respectively.
V
(L) represents for volume of initial solution and
m
(g) for mass of dry adsorbent.

In order to further observe the adsorption property, removal efficiency (R, %) of REEs was calculated using following Eq. (3):
(3)
R =
( C
0
-
C e
) ∗ 100
C 0

According to the result of optimizing the adding amount of CDs, ALG@CDs-3 was selected as the test adsorbent. Desorption process was carried out by immersing Gd (III)-adsorbed ALG@CDs-3 beads in a 100-mL conical flask containing 50 mL of 0.1 mol/L HCl for 3 h with the magnetic stirring violently. Repeat the above method several times until the concentration of Gd (III) in HCl solution could not be detected by ICP-OES at 336.2 nm. Afterwards, the ALG@CDs-3 beads were cleaned with 100 mL water for 3 h to remove H+ on the surface and repeated until the pH was neutral. The obtained adsorbents were filtered with filter paper and used for next Gd (III) adsorption as the previous concentration. Desorption experiment was conducted to 5 cycles continuously.

The competitive adsorption of ALG@CDs-3 beads was tested between target Gd (III) and non-target metal ions Al (III), Co (II), Ni (II), Zn (II), and Mg (II). For each test, 20 mg of the absorbent was added into a mixed solution containing Gd (III) and one of non-target metal ions. The concentration of each element in the mixed solution was 400 mg/L. The selectivity was calculated based on the ratio of adsorption amount on Gd (III) to the total adsorption amount.

Digital photos of undried ALG and ALG@CDs-3 beads were exhibited in Fig. S2(A, B) and the size distribution of them was shown in Fig. S2(C, D). The diameter of ALG@CDs-3 beads (3.2 mm) was slightly larger than that of ALG beads (2.9 mm), since CDs played a role in supporting the skeleton of alginate beads.

FTIR spectra with the range of 400–4000 cm−1 of ALG, ALG@CDs-3, Gd (III)-adsorbed ALG@CDs-3 beads, and CDs were given in Fig. 1 . For the infrared peaks of ALG, the stretching shaking of O–H was at 3300 cm−1, asymmetric and symmetrical vibration of –COO– were at 1591 cm−1 and 1413 cm−1, respectively, which was the same as that reported in the references [31,35], while the C–O stretching peak was at 1025 cm−1; and the C–H stretching vibration peak was at 2931 cm−1. After doping CDs, the peak of O–H stretching vibration overlapping with N–H stretching vibration shifted to 3248 cm−1, which suggested that CDs was successfully emerged into the framework of ALG beads. The obtained results were consistent with those previously reported [36,37]. After ALG@CDs-3 beads adsorbed Gd (III), the stretching vibration peak of N–H and O–H was changed to 3348 cm−1, and enhanced significantly, which was due to electrostatic interaction between the adsorbent and Gd (III). For the CDs, the peak of O–H stretching vibration was at 3433 cm−1, the peak of N–H stretching vibration was 3224 cm−1, and 2908 cm−1 was related to stretching vibration peak of C–H in –CH and –CH2. The results were consistent with those in the reference literature [33], and confirmed successful synthesis of the N-doped CDs.

The HRTEM images of CDs and SEM images of ALG and ALG@CDs-3 were shown in Fig. 2 . Seen from the SEM of ALG, it could be noted that uniform gaps appeared with relatively rough surface. Although CDs could not be characterized by SEM, the surface of ALG beads became smoother after doping CDs, and the CDs existence was truly obvious. The structure of the beads before and after embedding CDs was not visibly changed. Seen from the morphology of CDs, there was a spherical shape with an average diameter of 7.3 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.28 nm corresponding to the (0 2 0) facet of graphite [38]. The zeta potential of the prepared CDs with positive charge density was 7.9 mV (Fig. S3).

XPS spectrum of the ALG, ALG@CDs-3 and Gd (III)-adsorbed ALG@CDs-3 was applied to observe adsorption mechanism of REEs. The presence of Gd 3d and Gd 4d peaks in a wide scan spectrum in Fig. 3 A indicated that Gd (III) was successfully bounded to ALG@CDs-3. In the high resolution Gd 4d spectrum (Fig. 3B) of ALG@CDs-3, there were two peaks at 143.0 eV (Gd 4d5/2) and 149.2 eV (Gd 4d3/2), which was consistent with Shi et al’s report. [39]. The XPS results further confirmed adsorption of Gd (III).

The high resolution N 1s XPS spectrum of ALG@CDs-3 was shown in Fig. 3C. N 1s spectrum before Gd (III) adsorption could be splitted into three sections at 399.4 eV, 400.6 eV, and 401.8 eV related to pyridine N, graphitic N, and amino N, respectively, which were belonging to N-doped CDs [40,41]. The three main peaks were shifted to the higher energy 399.8 eV, 400.7 eV, and 402.1 eV, respectively, and a reduced strength of N 1s could be evidently observed. These results were a powerful proof that N-doped CDs were a positive participant in the adsorption process.

Ca 2p XPS spectra of ALG@CDs-3 before and after Gd (III) adsorption were exhibited in Fig. 3D. The binding energy was at 347.7 eV for Ca 2p3/2 and at 351.2 eV for Ca 2p1/2 [42]. The intensity of both peaks dropped significantly after assimilating Gd (III), indicating that there was an exchange between Ca (III) and Gd (III) in adsorption process.

The XPS spectrum of O 1s exhibited two fitted peaks at 531.5 and 532.9 eV in Fig. 3E, which were pointed to C=O and C–O/C-OH functional groups [43] and transferred to 531.7 and 533.0 eV, respectively after Gd (III) adsorption. As shown in Fig. 3F, C 1s peaks of ALG@CDs-3 situated at 204.6, 285.1, 286.5, and 288.2 eV could be ascribed to C–C, C–O, C–O–C, and C=O bonds, respectively. The C–O and C=O bond slightly moved to 285.3 and 288.4 eV, respectively, revealing that the adsorption of Gd (III) was involved in oxygen-containing functional groups. The XPS results were in accordance with those shown in FT-IR. The XPS peaks of N 1s, O 1s, C 1s, and Ca 2p were the same as those previously reported [40–43]. FTIR and XPS spectrum jointly revealed the presence of functional group (–OH, –COOH, –NH2) in ALG@CDs-3 beads. A possible adsorption mechanism was involved in electrostatic adsorption and complexation of Gd (III) with the OH, NH2 and COOH groups in the sorbent.

Thermogravimetry curves of CDs, ALG, and ALG@CDs-3 were obtained by heating at a rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen atmosphere (Fig. 4 ). The ALG and ALG@CDs-3 possessed a two-stage decreasing tendency, while the CDs only had one decreasing trend. The first stage of weight consumption on ALG and ALG@CDs-3 was occurred between 40 °C and 200 °C with 11.32% and 16.05% weight losses. It was due to an evaporation of adsorption water on the surface and inside of the material. Subsequently, the second reduction was occurred between 200 °C and 800 °C accompanied by 55.90% and 53.56% weight losses, respectively, which was mainly due to the degradation of substantial functional groups. Only one weight loss step was occurred on the CDs from 40 °C to 400 °C with 90.07% weight consumption, due to the gradual elimination of the surface functional groups of CDs. A slight weight loss from 40 °C to 200 °C also suggested that the CDs were stable under 200 °C. ALG@CDs-3 lost more weight than ALG, owning to the N-doping CDs existed in it. It was also proved that the CDs were successfully doped into the ALG beads.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the ALG@CDs-3 bead dosage
ce:section.ce:para: Varied amounts of ALG@CDs-3 beads (5, 10, 15, 20 mg) were added to 10 mL of 600 mg/L Gd (III) solution. Influence of the dosage was explained in Fig. 5 A. Removal efficiency was increased as the mass from 5 to 20 mg, nevertheless the adsorption capacity was increased slightly and then decreased. The reason of the adsorption capacity increasing slightly first and then decreasing might be that adsorption active sites increased but the adsorption effect limited due to adsorbent assembling. Overall considering the adsorption amount and the adsorbent consumption, the optimal dosage was 20 mg and selected for following adsorption experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the initial pH on adsorption efficiency of Gd (III)
ce:section.ce:para: pH is the most critical factor in adsorption experiments. In order to investigate the influence of pH on adsorption efficiency, the ALG@CDs-3 beads were immersed in 10 mL of 400 mg/L Gd (III) solution with different initial pH values from 2 to 7. It was found that the adsorption capacity was gradually increased from 100.02 mg/g at pH 2 to 163.32 mg/g at pH 5, and then slightly decreased down to 158.92 mg/g at pH 7 (Fig. 5B). The main reason was the change of H+ concentration in the solution. Protonation of –NH2, –OH, and –COOH functional groups on the surface of the adsorbent was occurred at a lower pH. In addition, high H+ concentration aggravated competitive effect with Gd (III) by binding with the active sites [44]. In our work, a slight decrease in adsorption capacity appeared when pH was 7 because Gd (III) could combine with –OH in solution above pH 6.5 to form an incompatible substance Gd(OH)3 as the previously reported [45]. Based on the above analysis, pH 5.0 was selected as the initial solution pH for following adsorption experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of doping amounts of CDs and initial concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The four alginate adsorbents were shown in Fig. 6 A. The adsorption capacity generally raised with the increase of Gd (III) initial concentration, and finally reached the balance with appearance of the maximum adsorption amount. The reason was that adsorption sites of the adsorbent were not occupied wholly at low concentration, resulting in an enhanced adsorption performance with the increasing initial concentration before 600 mg/g. Adsorption sites were gradually occupied to be saturated, and it was no longer affected by solution concentration. The maximum adsorption capacity was 123.60 mg/g for ALG beads and 173.07 mg/g for ALG@CDs-1 beads, indicating that introducing amino groups could provide more coordination sites for Gd (III) adsorption. As the concentration of immersed CDs was increased, the maximum adsorption capacity of ALG@CDs-3 was increased to 201.70 mg/g. However, the maximum adsorption capacity was significantly reduced to 187.88 mg/g when the CDs were increased to 10 mL, because the excess CDs could occupy a large amount of hydroxyl and carboxyl sites in the ALG beads, which made it impossible to bind to Gd (III). The results indicated that introducing a suitable amount of N-doped CDs could improve the adsorption capacity of ALG@CDs for Gd (III).

Adsorption data of the four materials were applied to the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models, which are frequently used to explore interaction between adsorbate and adsorbent as following Eqs. (4) and (5) [46,47]:
(4)
C e
Q e
=
1
K l
Q
max
+
C e
Q
max
(5)
l g
Q e
= l g
K f
+
l g
C e
n
where
C e
(mg/L),
Q e
(mg/g) are REEs concentrations and adsorption capacity at equilibrium time respectively, and
Q
max
(mg/g) represents for maximum theoretical adsorption capacity.
K l
(L/mg),
K f
(L/mg),
n
are Langmuir and Freundlich empirical constants associated with the ability of adsorption.

The adsorption equilibrium isotherm data were fitted to both Langmuir and Freundlich models; subsequently, these plots and detailed relevant parameters were revealed in Fig. 6(B and C) and Table 1 . Generally understanding, Langmuir model predicts that monolayer adsorption process happens on a homogenous surface with identical active adsorption sites independently. Contrarily, Freundlich model predicts multilayer adsorption process that appears on a heterogeneous surface with unsaturated adsorption sites [47]. It was obviously found that Gd (III) adsorption was more suitable to the Langmuir model (R2 = 0.9991–0.9999) than to Freundlich model (R2 = 0.8546–0.9445). Hence, each Gd (III) ion was involved in a single adsorption site with the equal energy.
Q
max
values obtained from the Langmuir model of ALG, ALG@CDs-1, ALG@CDs-3, and ALG@CDs-10 were 124.38, 173.91, 201.21, and 188.32 mg/g, respectively, which were consistent with the results of equilibrium adsorption capacity. Gd (III) adsorption was obviously improved after doping CDs by introducing amino groups. The results showed that ALG@CDs hydrogel beads were an effective adsorbent for adsorbing REE ions.

At the same time, the dimensionless
R l
in the Langmuir model was also used to observe the adsorption behavior as following Eq. (6) [46]:
(6)
R l
=
1
1 +
K l
C 0
where
C 0
(mg/L) represents for initial concentration. When calculated
R l
is between 0 and 1, it is beneficial for adsorption process.

CDs embedded in alginate hydrogel beads were easy to be separated from the REEs solution after adsorption, and promote the adsorption capacity of alginate hydrogel beads. Obtained alginate-carbon dots composites (ALG@CDs) beads could be used as a convenient absorbent.

The adsorption of other REEs Dy (III), Sm (III), and Pr (III) on ALG@CDs-3 beads was also investigated with a various initial concentration as shown in Fig. 6D. The maximum adsorption capacity was increased with the rising atomic number as a consequence of lanthanide contraction [48]. The REE ions with large ordinals possess a thicker charge density with a smaller radius and had higher ability to combine with ALG@CDs-3 beads compared with the small ordinals. The adsorption equilibrium isotherm data of Dy (III), Sm (III), and Pr (III) were shown in Fig. 6(E and F) and Table 2 for details. Their isotherm models were also fitted to Langmuir monolayer adsorption (R2 = 0.9983–0.9996). The adsorption capacities of ALG@CDs-3 for Pr (III), Sm (III), and Dy (III) were 172.16, 184.89, and 206.15 mg/g, respectively, according to the maximum theoretical adsorption capacity of Langmuir model, which matched well with the experimental data. The results indicated that the ALG@CDs-3 beads also had an efficient adsorption for other rare earth elements.

The Gd (III) adsorption performance on ALG@CDs-3 beads with increasing time was shown in Fig. 7 A. At beginning, the rate of adsorption rose sharply with increasing time, then ascended slowly, and finally reached equilibrium within 18 h. The main reason was ascribed that Gd (III) ions were rapidly combined with the surface groups of ALG@CDs-3 beads; later the Gd (III) in the outer solution slowly penetrated into the interior of the adsorbents to occupy their active sites; finally, the sites were saturated, which were hard to capture the remaining ions. On the other hand, as Gd (III) was gradually enriched in the beads, the repulsive force of the Gd (III) in the contact solution was also gradually increased, resulting in a weakness of the adsorption rate. In order to get deep insights into the adsorption kinetics and elucidate the rate-controlling step of the adsorption process, the adsorption data were fitted to classic kinetics models pseudo-first-order (PFO) and pseudo-second-order (PSO) as following Eqs. (7) and (8) [49,50]:
(7)
ln
Q e
-
Q t
= ln
Q e
-
K 1
t
(8)
t
Q t
=
1
K 2
Q
e
2
+
t
Q e
where
K 1
(/min) and
K 2
(g/mg/min) are rate constants of PFO and PSO models, respectively.

The adsorption data were fitted to both the PFO and PSO models. Afterwards, these plots were shown in Fig. 7(B and C) and detailed kinetics parameters were summarized in Table 3 . The R2-correlation linear coefficient of PSO model (R2 = 0.9918) was close to 1, compared with the PFO model (R2 = 0.9851). The adsorption capacity computed from the PSO model was close to the experimental data. Thus, fitted results showed that PSO model was proper for describing the adsorption process of Gd (III) on ALG@CDs-3 beads, which indicated that the rate-controlling step mainly depended on chemical adsorption involved in electronic sharing and transferring between the adsorbent and adsorbate.

To explore the changes and feasibility of energy in the adsorption process, some thermodynamic functions, such as enthalpy (ΔH), entropy (ΔS), and free energy (ΔG) change, were computed on basis of adsorption capacity (Fig. 8 A) at different temperatures (298.15, 303.15, 308.15, 313.15 K) with 400 mg/L Gd (III) solution. Free energy change (ΔG) was calculated as the following Eq. (9), and ΔS and ΔH were estimated by the slope and the intercept of the Van’t Hoff equation as following Eq. (10) [50]:
(9)
Δ G = - R T ln
Q e
C e
(10)
Δ G = Δ H - T Δ S
where T (K) is the Kelvin temperature and R (8.314 J mol−1 K−1) is the gas constant.

The dotted line diagram of the Van’t Hoff equation was shown in Fig. 8B and detailed thermodynamic parameters were listed in Table 4 . From thermodynamic data, it was found that the ΔG was negative under the condition of all the temperatures, indicating that the adsorption process was deemed to a spontaneous process. Meanwhile, the reduction of ΔG value with the increasing temperature revealed that high temperature played an energetic role. Moreover, the negative value ΔH similarly indicated that the process was endothermic process. The ΔH is also frequently used to distinguish whether physical or chemical adsorption. For physical adsorption, its value falls in the range of 2.1–20.9 kJ/mol, while for chemical adsorption, the value is in the range of 20.9–418.4 kJ/mol. The calculated ΔH (25.00 kJ/mol) was belonged to chemical adsorption, which was consistent with the kinetic conclusion [51]. When ΔS value is positive, the randomness of solid/liquid interface increases [52].

The regeneration experiment was conducted to ALG@CDs-3 beads in order to veritably apply to the treatment of environmental waste water. The Gd (III)-adsorbed beads were treated repeatedly with 0.1 mol/L hydrochloric acid as the aforementioned method, and the adsorption capacity after adsorption and desorption five times was shown in Fig. 9 . Between the first and second cycles, the adsorption amount had a certain decrease, owing to the eluent H+ destroying the active sites on the adsorbent surface. After five cycles, the adsorption performance to Gd (III) still reached 117.10 mg/g, meanwhile, the regeneration efficiency was maintained above 73.60%, suggesting that ALG@CDs-3 beads were an excellent, economical, and renewable adsorbent with practical potential for REE adsorption.

Since REE ions generally coexist with transition metal ions in REEs-containing minerals and industrial waste water, it is required to research the selectivity of adsorbents. The selectivity result was shown in Fig. 10 . The selectivity of ALG@CDs beads was obvious for Gd (III). The Gd (III) competition for bivalent ions Co (II), Ni (II), Mg (II), and Zn (II) was totally over 80%, while the competitiveness of trivalent ions Al (III) was only about 70%. The main reason was that high-valent metal ions were more favorable for binding to adsorption sites than the low-valent according to the previous reports [53]. The results indicated that ALG@CDs beads could be potential selective adsorption material for REEs.

In conclusion, SA composite hydrogel beads decorated with amino groups could be rationally introduced by N-doped CDs, leading to highly enhance REE adsorption. Under the optimized condition, ALG@CDs beads had excellent adsorption capacity for Gd (III) (up to 201.21 mg/g), compared with bare ALG beads (124.38 mg/g). It also could adsorb other REEs Pr (III), Sm (III), and Dy (III) with the capacity of 172.16, 184.89, and 206.15 mg/g, respectively. The adsorption process satisfied the Langmuir isotherm model (R2 > 0.9991) and pseudo-second-order kinetic model (R2 = 0.9918), which indicated that the process was involved in rate-controlling chemical adsorption on homogenous monolayer surface. Thermodynamic investigation revealed that temperature was a positive factor for the spontaneous adsorption process. By the means of doping a small amount of CDs, the adsorption efficiency of SA beads to REEs was greatly improved. The regeneration beads could be easily collected and reused for five adsorption-regeneration cycles. As a result, the ALG@CDs beads could be marked as an environmental-friendly and effective adsorbent, opening up a new potential industrialization method to adsorb REEs in waste water.

Zhiwei Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Quan Li: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Zhiyue Li: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Cong Liu: Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Xuerui Liu: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Yuanyuan Liu: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Genlai Donga: Writing - review & editing. Tao Lan: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Yun Wei: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.12.030.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms to predict adsorbate removal efficiency or required amount of adsorbent
